skill.name: minimax-pdf
skill.description: Create, fill, and reformat polished PDFs with a token-based design system for covers, body pages, tables, charts, forms, and print-ready output.
skill.summary: Generate polished PDFs, fill PDF forms, and restyle existing documents.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported a 100/100 risk score, but most critical and high alerts are heuristic false positives from Markdown examples, design vocabulary, color hex values, and terminal-style labels. Real risks remain: the skill executes local scripts, can auto-install dependencies, writes files, loads remote fonts or images, and renders unescaped user metadata in local HTML via Playwright. No evidence found of malicious intent, credential theft, prompt injection, or deliberate data exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Automatic Dependency Installation Executes Package Managers
medium_findings.description: The skill can install Python and Node dependencies through pip, npm, and npx. This is legitimate setup behavior, but it executes package-manager code from external registries and should be run only in trusted environments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The package-manager commands are directly visible and are reachable through check, fix, and dependency bootstrap paths. The behavior supports the PDF workflow, so the risk is operational rather than confirmed malicious.

medium_findings.title: Unescaped User Metadata Rendered as Local HTML
medium_findings.description: Cover generation interpolates user-controlled title, subtitle, author, abstract, and image URL values into HTML without escaping before Playwright renders the file. A crafted value could alter cover HTML or execute browser-side script during rendering.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple f-string insertions place metadata directly into HTML attributes or elements. The browser context limits impact, but the injection vector is clear.

medium_findings.title: Remote Resource Loading During PDF Rendering
medium_findings.description: The cover renderer can load Google Fonts and caller-provided cover images while rendering HTML to PDF. This creates outbound network requests and can disclose render timing or requested asset URLs.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The Google Fonts URL and image src interpolation are explicit. No credential exfiltration was found, but network access is part of normal rendering.

medium_findings.title: Broad Local File Read and Write Capabilities
medium_findings.description: The workflow reads user-selected PDFs, Markdown, JSON, images, and generated intermediate files, then writes output PDFs and JSON. This is expected for a PDF tool, but untrusted inputs should be handled in a sandbox.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are direct and necessary for the skill. The risk depends on trust in input files and the runtime environment.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Alerts Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Many external-command hits in README.md, SKILL.md, and design/design.md are fenced command examples or inline filenames. They document how to run the skill and are not executed by those Markdown files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown documentation or code fences, not executable script bodies. Actual command execution is separately captured in script findings.

low_findings.title: Keyword and Weak-Crypto Heuristics Are Mostly Design Vocabulary
low_findings.description: Critical and high keyword alerts such as SYSTEM_REPORT, SYSTEM_FALLBACK, terminal, and color-like hex strings are design labels or constants. I found no evidence that these represent C2 logic, Windows SAM access, or weak cryptographic use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context shows palette tables, fallback font constants, and terminal-themed cover labels. These are semantic false positives rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Form Filling Uses Expected PDF Library Operations
low_findings.description: The static sensitive-file and filesystem alerts around PDF form inspection and writing map to pypdf operations on caller-provided files. No evidence found of hidden credential discovery or unauthorized filesystem traversal.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code checks input existence, parses JSON values, and writes the requested output path. The behavior is consistent with a form-filling tool.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Manager Auto-Install
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill can invoke pip, npm, and npx automatically for missing dependencies. This pattern should be disclosed because it executes external installation code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and directly tied to dependency setup. The risk is real even though it supports legitimate functionality.

dangerous_patterns.title: HTML Generation Without Escaping
dangerous_patterns.description: User metadata is interpolated into generated cover HTML without escaping. This can permit cover HTML injection during local Playwright rendering.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The f-string insertions are direct and no escaping helper is present in the cited code path. Impact depends on the rendering sandbox.

dangerous_patterns.title: Outbound Asset Loading
dangerous_patterns.description: Google Fonts imports and optional image URLs can trigger network requests while rendering a PDF cover.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL construction and HTML injection are explicit. No code path showed credential attachment to those requests.

content.user_title: Create Polished PDFs with a Design System
content.value_statement: Generic PDFs waste time and weaken professional communication. This skill creates, fills, and reformats PDFs with consistent visual design.
actual_capabilities: Generates new PDFs from structured content using cover, body, and merge scripts.
Creates visual identities through document types, accent colors, typography, and spacing tokens.
Fills existing PDF form fields after inspecting available field names and choices.
Reformats Markdown, text, JSON, or extracted PDF text into the create pipeline.
Renders tables, callouts, images, charts, math, flowcharts, code blocks, and bibliographies.
limitations: Requires Python, Node.js, ReportLab, pypdf, Playwright, and Chromium for full output.
Cover fonts and optional cover images can require network access during rendering.
PDF reformatting uses best-effort text extraction and may not preserve original layout.
Generated cover HTML does not escape all user metadata before Playwright rendering.

use_cases.title: Build Client-Ready Reports
use_cases.description: Create polished strategy reports, proposals, and summaries with matching cover and body design.

use_cases.title: Complete PDF Forms
use_cases.description: Inspect fillable PDF fields, map exact field names, and write validated values into a new PDF.

use_cases.title: Restyle Existing Documents
use_cases.description: Convert Markdown, text, JSON, or extracted PDF text into a more consistent designed PDF.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a polished PDF report titled Annual Operations Review. Use a professional report style and include sections for summary, metrics, risks, and next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Fill a PDF Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect this PDF form, list the available fields, then fill the fields I provide and save the completed PDF separately.

prompt_templates.title: Reformat a Draft Document
prompt_templates.prompt: Reformat this Markdown document into a client-ready PDF. Choose a suitable document type, accent color, and cover treatment based on the content.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Data-Heavy Proposal
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a proposal PDF with an executive cover, section hierarchy, comparison tables, callouts, charts, a process flow, and a bibliography.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a Q3 strategy review PDF with an executive tone.
output_examples.output: A multi-page report with a designed cover, consistent body typography, section rules, callouts, tables, and final PDF merge checks.

output_examples.input: A user uploads a fillable onboarding form and provides employee details.
output_examples.output: A completed PDF form saved as a new file, with a summary of filled fields and any missing field names.

output_examples.input: A user provides a rough Markdown proposal draft.
output_examples.output: A restyled PDF proposal with parsed sections, cleaned typography, a matching cover, and print-ready page structure.

best_practices: Run the dependency check before generating production PDFs.
Inspect PDF forms before filling them so field names and choice values are exact.
Review reformat output when source PDFs contain complex layouts or scanned pages.
anti_patterns: Do not run automatic dependency installation in a restricted production environment.
Do not pass untrusted HTML-like metadata into cover titles, subtitles, or abstracts.
Do not expect PDF reformatting to preserve precise layout from the original file.

faq.question: Can this skill create a PDF from scratch?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses a create pipeline with design tokens, cover HTML, body rendering, and PDF merging.

faq.question: Can it fill existing PDF forms?
faq.answer: Yes. It can inspect fields first, then write values into text, checkbox, dropdown, and radio fields.

faq.question: Can it reformat an existing PDF?
faq.answer: Yes, but PDF parsing is best-effort. It extracts text and rebuilds the document through the create pipeline.

faq.question: Does it need network access?
faq.answer: Sometimes. Google Fonts, optional cover images, and dependency installation can require network access.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report marks it as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive documents?
faq.answer: Use caution. It processes local files and may load remote fonts or images unless the workflow is restricted.
